strategy_summary: AquaLoop should be treated as a hospitality water-reuse validation system, not a proven product. The recommended path is assessment-to-pilot, with client-ready claims blocked until buyer, technical, regulatory, economic, operational, manufacturing, and legal evidence is validated.
verifier_readiness_summary: Package is internal-draft-ready for agency review and web deck preview. Client-ready delivery is blocked by unresolved buyer validation, product architecture, technical feasibility, regulatory path, installation and maintenance model, property economics, manufacturing feasibility, and brand/legal review.

slides.title: AquaLoop should be framed as a hospitality water-reuse validation system
slides.purpose: Introduce the executive thesis and establish evidence discipline.
slides.key_message: The most credible near-term strategy is assessment-to-pilot, not a claim that AquaLoop is a finished hotel greywater product.

content_blocks.heading: Executive thesis
content_blocks.body: Hospitality is the leading validation wedge, but AquaLoop must earn product, code, economics, and buyer claims through proof.

content_blocks.heading: Strategy move
body: Sell decision confidence before selling a system.
Use one property loop to validate source, demand, code, architecture, and operations.
Keep savings, ROI, certification, and installability claims blocked until measured.

slides.visual_direction: Dark editorial title card with oversized slide number, pale blue thesis panel, and visible readiness badges.
slides.speaker_notes: Open by making the recommendation precise: the opportunity is serious, but the product is not yet proven.
production_notes: Do not show finished hardware or fake installation imagery.
slides.needs_input_reason: The thesis depends on unvalidated buyer, technical, regulatory, economic, operational, and legal evidence.

slides.title: Water reuse is becoming a building strategy issue, but claims must stay local and proven
slides.purpose: Ground the opportunity in public category evidence without overclaiming AquaLoop readiness.
slides.key_message: Onsite non-potable reuse is a recognized category, but approval and claims depend on local rules, source water, end use, treatment, and operations.

content_blocks.heading: Category
content_blocks.body: Onsite non-potable reuse is documented by public and industry sources.

content_blocks.heading: Code
content_blocks.body: Jurisdiction comes first: source, end use, treatment, monitoring, and operations vary locally.

content_blocks.heading: Claim rule
content_blocks.body: Standards and local programs are pathways to investigate, not AquaLoop compliance proof.

slides.visual_direction: Off-white slide with three large pale blue cards and a compact evidence footer.
slides.speaker_notes: Use this slide to show why the market deserves attention while setting a rigorous proof boundary.
production_notes: Keep local-rule caveat visible in the card text.

slides.title: Hospitality is the strongest first wedge to validate, not a proven launch market
slides.purpose: Explain why hotels lead the current discovery recommendation.
slides.key_message: Hotels concentrate repeated water-use contexts, operational stakeholders, and sustainability narrative, but no hotel economics or buyer willingness has been proven.

content_blocks.heading: Why hospitality rises
body: Repeated water-use contexts make source-to-use discovery plausible.
Operations and facilities teams create a path to technical feedback.
Sustainability programs give water stewardship a narrative layer.

content_blocks.heading: Why it is still unproven
body: No target property economics are available.
No buyer interviews have confirmed budget ownership.
No retrofit or code pathway has been validated.

slides.visual_direction: Pale blue opportunity panel beside a proof-needed stack.
slides.speaker_notes: Hold the line between plausible and proven.
production_notes: Avoid market size, ROI, or water-savings metrics.

slides.title: Segment priority depends on source-demand fit, buyer access, and installability
slides.purpose: Compare hospitality against other candidate first markets.
slides.key_message: Hospitality leads the current matrix, while multifamily/new-build and student housing remain credible second paths if retrofit feasibility or buyer access fails.

body.proof_needed: Property economics, buyer interviews, code path

slides.visual_direction: Clean matrix with pale blue lead-wedge row and amber validation labels.
slides.speaker_notes: Show that the hotel recommendation was challenged against alternatives.
production_notes: Label segment priority as internal hypothesis.
slides.needs_input_reason: Segment scoring is an internal hypothesis without buyer interviews, property data, or economic modeling.

slides.title: The hotel buying decision is a chain, not a single persona
slides.purpose: Map the likely buying and veto structure for hotel pilots.
slides.key_message: AquaLoop likely needs owner sponsorship, operations acceptance, facilities approval, MEP review, and code confidence before a pilot can proceed.

content_blocks.heading: Decision chain
content_blocks.body: Owner or asset sponsor -> operations sponsor -> facilities technical veto -> MEP/code review -> pilot approval.

content_blocks.heading: Validation required
body: Confirm budget owner and approval path.
Identify who can veto installation or maintenance burden.
Learn which proof is required before capex or pilot approval.

slides.visual_direction: Stakeholder chain map with veto markers and proof-needed badges.
slides.speaker_notes: Make buyer validation a workstream, not an afterthought.
production_notes: Add interview quotes only after buyer research.
slides.needs_input_reason: Buyer chain, budget owner, and approval path require hotel buyer, facilities, MEP, and operator interviews.

slides.title: Product architecture should stay options-based until property and code inputs narrow it
slides.purpose: Compare architecture options without selecting an unsupported product path.
slides.key_message: Per-room, centralized, semi-centralized, retrofit, new-build, and managed-service models remain possible; the first sprint should eliminate options through evidence.

content_blocks.heading: Current recommendation
content_blocks.body: Compare centralized and semi-centralized paths first, but keep this as an engineering hypothesis.

slides.visual_direction: Large rounded architecture cards with validation status labels.
slides.speaker_notes: This is the product architecture slide, but its job is to preserve options until evidence narrows them.
production_notes: Do not show a polished device render.
Use validation required badges on all architecture options.
slides.needs_input_reason: No engineering feasibility, selected source/end-use loop, property survey, or launch jurisdiction exists.

slides.title: Technical feasibility hinges on source, treatment, storage, monitoring, and fail-safe design
slides.purpose: Surface technical unknowns that could break the product.
slides.key_message: The technical risk is the full operating system from source capture to fail-safe behavior, not only treatment technology.

content_blocks.heading: Unknowns
body: Source water quality and capture method.
Storage, pumps, controls, monitoring, and alarms.
Cross-connection, odor, stagnation, and fail-safe behavior.

content_blocks.heading: Proof needed
body: Instrument a pilot before making performance claims.

slides.visual_direction: Dark risk grid with pale blue technical proof callout.
slides.speaker_notes: Make technical uncertainty feel managed, not hidden.
production_notes: Do not imply treatment technology has been selected.
slides.needs_input_reason: Technical feasibility requires engineering review, standards consultation, source/end-use selection, and pilot validation.

slides.title: Installation and maintenance risk may decide the winning architecture
slides.purpose: Tie operational risk to product strategy and service model.
slides.key_message: Hotels may reject an efficient system if it disrupts guests, burdens facilities, or creates unclear service responsibility.

content_blocks.heading: Product implication
content_blocks.body: The service model may be part of the product, not an add-on.

slides.visual_direction: Dark cards with amber validation labels and a service-model callout.
slides.speaker_notes: Reframe maintenance as a buying-risk reducer.
production_notes: Use may and requires validation; no service-interval or install-cost claims.
slides.needs_input_reason: Installation and maintenance burden require site surveys, facilities interviews, engineering review, and service-model estimates.

slides.title: Competitors own generic greywater language, so AquaLoop needs proof-led specificity
slides.purpose: Explain competitive landscape and positioning implications.
slides.key_message: Named vendors and substitutes already occupy reuse territory; AquaLoop should differentiate through hospitality-specific, code-first validation.

body.examples: Epic Cleantec, Hydraloop, Greyter, Orbital Systems, INTEWA AQUALOOP, ReWater, Nexus eWater
Fixtures, conservation programs, leak detection, smart metering
body.implication: Prove why reuse is worth more complexity.

body.examples: Municipal water and sewer plus incremental conservation
body.implication: Show why change is worth the risk.

slides.visual_direction: Competitor territory map with vendor-source caveat and pale blue whitespace card.
slides.speaker_notes: Use competitors as evidence that the category is occupied, not as independently verified performance benchmarks.
production_notes: Vendor claims must be labeled as vendor self-positioning.
Add dated captures before formal external presentation.

slides.title: AquaLoop should own code-first hospitality reuse validation
slides.key_message: The credible story is to validate the right water loop for the property, not to sell a universal greywater system.
slides.visual_direction: Black positioning card paired with an off-white claim-control panel.
slides.speaker_notes: Give the team a memorable narrative without overclaiming.
production_notes: Keep this slide internal until buyer and legal validation.
slides.needs_input_reason: Positioning requires buyer validation, engineering/regulatory proof, and legal review of the AquaLoop name.

slides.title: The first MVP should validate one property loop before productizing the system
slides.purpose: Define the MVP and pilot strategy.
slides.key_message: The MVP should validate one source-to-use loop, one jurisdiction path, one architecture, and one buyer decision chain before commercial productization.
slides.visual_direction: Horizontal process timeline from baseline to pilot decision with proof-needed cards.
slides.speaker_notes: Make the next sprint concrete and evidence-driven.
production_notes: Do not show fake pilot metrics.
Use proof needed labels where metrics would eventually go.
slides.needs_input_reason: MVP scope requires selected property, launch geography, engineering partner, AHJ path, and pilot sponsor.

slides.title: Client-ready claims require a staged proof roadmap
slides.purpose: Convert blockers into a validation plan.
slides.key_message: Each desired claim has a proof gate; until the gate is passed, the claim stays internal, caveated, or blocked.

body.proof_needed: Water bills, tariffs, capex, opex, maintenance

slides.visual_direction: Proof ladder with dark blocked cards, pale blue opportunity cards, and readiness badges.
slides.speaker_notes: Show that the output is actionable even though client-ready claims are blocked.
production_notes: Keep roadmap internal until owners and budget are assigned.
slides.needs_input_reason: The roadmap is internal planning until validation owners, data sources, budget, and external claim approvals are confirmed.

slides.title: The package is internal-draft-ready, with external use blocked by named evidence gaps
slides.purpose: Close with readiness status and next actions.
slides.key_message: The package is useful for internal strategy and demo review, but client-ready delivery is blocked by technical, regulatory, buyer, economic, manufacturing, and legal gaps.

body.next_action: Use for strategy review and validation planning
Close buyer, technical, regulatory, economic, and legal gaps
Pick geography, property, interviews, and engineering review

slides.visual_direction: Readiness ledger with oversized slide number, badges, and exact blocked categories.
slides.speaker_notes: End with honest momentum and concrete next actions.
